section.title: The Words of Agur
section.sectionRationale: A distinct new voice speaks here, Agur son of Jakeh, otherwise unknown to Scripture, whose oracle opens with a confession of ignorance before God and moves through a prayer for honest daily bread, a string of vivid numerical sayings, and closes with a warning against self-exaltation and stirred up anger.
section.thesis: True wisdom begins by confessing how little we know before the incomprehensible God, asking for neither poverty nor riches but daily bread, and observing the world closely enough to see both the horror of certain evils and the humble genius written into ants, badgers, and locusts.
section.frameNote: Six clusters: Agur's confession of ignorance and the mystery of God and His Son; every word of God is flawless, and the prayer for honest bread; generations of evil and things never satisfied; wonders too great to understand, and the adulteress who feels no shame; small creatures who are exceedingly wise; stately creatures, and the folly of self-exaltation.

units.label: Agur's confession of ignorance
units.note: the mystery of God and His Son

verses.text:
These are the words of Agur son of Jakeh, the burden that this man declared to Ithiel: 
"I am weary, O God, and worn out.
Surely I am the most ignorant of men, and I lack the understanding of a man.
I have not learned wisdom, and I have no knowledge of the Holy One.

Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in His hands? Who has bound up the waters in His cloak? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is His name, and what is the name of His Son, surely you know!

units.label: Every word of God is flawless

verses.text: Every word of {{eloah|God}} is flawless; He is a shield to those who take refuge in Him.
Do not add to His words, lest He rebuke you and prove you a liar.
Two things I ask of You, do not refuse me before I die:

Keep falsehood and deceitful words far from me. Give me neither poverty nor riches; 
feed me with the bread that is my portion.
Otherwise, I may have too much and deny You, saying, 'Who is the LORD?' Or I may become poor and steal, profaning the name of my God.
Do not slander a servant to his master, or he will curse you, and you will bear the guilt.
There is a generation of those who curse their fathers and do not bless their mothers.
There is a generation of those who are pure in their own eyes and yet unwashed of their filth.
There is a generation, how haughty are their eyes and pretentious are their glances,
there is a generation whose teeth are swords and whose jaws are knives, devouring the oppressed from the earth and the needy from among men.

The leech has two daughters: Give and Give. 
There are three things that are never satisfied, four that never say, 'Enough!':

Sheol, 
the barren womb, 
land never satisfied with water, 
and fire that never says, 'Enough!'

units.label: Wonders too great to understand
units.note: the adulteress who feels no shame

verses.text: As for the eye that mocks a father and scorns obedience to a mother, may the ravens of the valley pluck it out and young vultures devour it.
There are three things too wonderful for me, four that I cannot understand:

the way of an eagle in the sky, 
the way of a snake on a rock, 
the way of a ship at sea, 
and the way of a man with a {{almah|maiden}}.

This is the way of an adulteress: She eats and wipes her mouth 
and says, 'I have done nothing wrong.'

units.label: Small creatures, exceedingly wise
units.note: the earth trembles under four things

verses.text: Under three things the earth trembles, under four it cannot bear up:

a servant who becomes king, 
a fool who is filled with food,

an unloved woman who marries, 
and a maidservant who supplants her mistress.
Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly {{chakam|wise}}:
The ants are creatures of little strength, yet they store up their food in the summer;
the rock badgers are creatures of little power, yet they make their homes in the rocks;
the locusts have no king, yet they all advance in formation;
and the lizard can be caught in one's hands, yet it is found in the palaces of kings.

units.label: Stately creatures and self-exaltation
units.note: the stirring of anger brings forth strife

verses.text: There are three things that are stately in their stride, and four that are impressive in their walk:
a lion, mighty among beasts, refusing to retreat before anything;

If you have foolishly exalted yourself or if you have plotted evil, 
put your hand over your mouth.

For as the churning of milk yields butter, and the twisting of the nose draws blood, 
so the stirring of anger brings forth strife."

eloah.gloss: God, a singular form related to Elohim, often used in poetic and wisdom literature, notably throughout Job. Verse 5 says every word of this God is flawless, a shield to those who take refuge in Him, grounding Agur's confessed ignorance, verse 3, in trust that God's own revealed word can be relied on completely even when human understanding cannot.

almah.gloss: A young woman of marriageable age, maiden. Verse 19 lists the way of a man with an almah among wonders too great to understand, the same Hebrew word famously used in Isaiah 7:14 of the virgin who would bear Immanuel, showing how ordinary human wonder and covenant promise can share a single word.

chakam.gloss: Wise, skilled in living rightly. Verse 24 calls the ant, the rock badger, the locust, and the lizard 'exceedingly wise' despite their smallness or weakness, teaching that true wisdom is measured by fittingness to one's calling and diligence, not by size, strength, or status.

basic.context: A new and distinct voice speaks in this chapter, Agur son of Jakeh, who is otherwise unknown in Scripture. He opens with a startling confession of his own ignorance before the incomprehensible God, prays simply for honest daily bread rather than poverty or riches, and then teaches through a series of vivid numerical riddles, three or four things, that train the eye to see both the horror of certain evils and the humble wisdom written into small creatures.
basic.hermeneutics: Agur's confessed ignorance, verse 2 to 3, is not false modesty but the honest starting point of real wisdom, since he immediately turns to trust God's flawless word, verse 5, rather than his own understanding. The numerical sayings, three things, four things, are a poetic device common in ancient wisdom literature, building suspense and listing examples that share a common feature, not a rigid mathematical formula.
basic.originalLanguages: Agur names God eloah, verse 5, the same poetic name used often in Job, and confesses total confidence in His flawless word even while confessing his own ignorance of the Holy One, verse 3. The almah, young woman, of verse 19, is the identical Hebrew word Isaiah later uses of the virgin who will bear Immanuel, 7:14, though here it simply names the wonder of a man's courtship, a linguistic link worth noting without overclaiming a direct prophecy in this context.
basic.typology: Agur's rhetorical question, 'what is His name, and what is the name of His Son,' verse 4, anticipates the fuller revelation of God the Father and God the Son that Scripture only later makes explicit, though Agur himself could not have grasped the incarnation. His prayer for neither poverty nor riches, verse 8, foreshadows Jesus' own teaching to pray for daily bread, not accumulated wealth, Matthew 6:11.
basic.christ: Agur's haunting question, 'what is His name, and what is the name of His Son, surely you know,' verse 4, stands as one of the Old Testament's most striking anticipations of the Father-Son relationship within God, fully unveiled only in the incarnation of Jesus Christ, the Son who alone has made the Father known, John 1:18. And Agur's simple prayer for daily bread rather than poverty or riches, verse 8, echoes forward into the Lord's Prayer, where Jesus teaches His disciples to ask their Father for exactly this, 'give us this day our daily bread,' Matthew 6:11.
basic.god: God is the Holy One whom no human wisdom can fully grasp, verse 3, yet every word He speaks is utterly flawless and a shield to those who take refuge in Him, verse 5. He is also the giver of daily bread who hears the simple prayer of a man who wants neither the temptation of riches nor the desperation of poverty, verse 8, showing His care extends to the ordinary, daily needs of His people.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:18 answers Agur's question about the name of God's Son. Matthew 6:11 echoes the prayer for daily bread in verse 8. Job 38:4-11 parallels the rhetorical questions of verse 4 about who has measured creation. Psalm 18:30 echoes the flawless word of God as a shield, verse 5.
basic.oneStory: Agur confesses that true wisdom begins in humility before the incomprehensible God, trusts God's flawless word completely, and asks simply for daily bread rather than poverty or riches. His unanswered question about the name of God's Son finds its answer centuries later in Jesus Christ, the Son who alone reveals the Father and teaches His people to pray for exactly the bread Agur asked for.

context.literary: Chapter 30 stands apart as its own labeled oracle, 'the words of Agur son of Jakeh,' distinct in voice and form from both the earlier Solomonic collections and the closing words of Lemuel in chapter 31, built around a personal confession, a prayer, and a series of numerical riddles.
context.historical: Agur and Ithiel are otherwise unknown figures, and their names, along with the reference to Jakeh, have prompted various proposals about their origin, though nothing certain can be established beyond what the text itself states; the numerical riddle form, three things... four things, was a recognized wisdom convention across the ancient Near East.
context.canonical: Agur's rhetorical question about the name of God's Son, verse 4, stands remarkably alone in the Old Testament as an explicit reference to a divine Son alongside God Himself, a thread that remains largely dormant until the New Testament's full revelation of the Trinity.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model humble confession of human ignorance before God, to teach dependence on God's flawless word and simple provision rather than self-sufficiency, and to sharpen observation of the world through vivid numerical sayings that expose both grievous evil and hidden wisdom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Agur's prayer, verses 7 to 9, is a direct petition modeling right desire, while most of the numerical sayings, verses 11 to 31, are observational, describing categories of things that share a feature, from the insatiable to the small yet wise to the stately, inviting reflection rather than issuing direct commands.

debates.question: Is Agur's question in verse 4, 'what is His name, and what is the name of His Son,' a direct prophecy of the Trinity and the incarnate Son of God?

views.label: A direct, intentional messianic prophecy of the divine Son
views.case: Some read the parallel between God's name and 'His Son's' name as Agur, under inspiration, glimpsing the plurality within God's own being that the New Testament later reveals fully in the Father and the Son.

views.label: A rhetorical device asserting God's incomprehensibility, later filled with fuller meaning
views.case: In context, Agur's string of questions, who has ascended to heaven, gathered the wind, bound the waters, functions rhetorically to establish that no human, and by extension no human's son, has done these things, only God; the phrase most likely functioned in its original setting as an assertion that God's identity and being are beyond any human's or human family's grasp, a meaning Scripture's later revelation of the Father and the Son fills with unforeseen depth without requiring Agur to have understood the Trinity explicitly.

debates.bearing: Whichever reading is preferred, the New Testament's later revelation of the Father and the Son, John 1:18, gives Agur's ancient, deliberately unanswerable question its truest and fullest answer, an answer Agur himself could not have supplied but that the whole of Scripture was moving toward.

study.originalLanguages: Agur addresses eloah, God, verse 5, confessing that every word this God speaks is flawless, a trustworthy shield even for one, like Agur himself, verse 3, who confesses lacking knowledge of the Holy One. The almah, young woman, of verse 19, is the same Hebrew term Isaiah later uses of the virgin bearing Immanuel, 7:14, though here Agur simply marvels at the mystery of human courtship, one of several things too wonderful to fully explain. And chakam, wise, verse 24, is applied to small, weak creatures, ants, badgers, locusts, and lizards, redefining wisdom as fittingness and diligence rather than size or strength.

typology.type: The unanswered question of God's Son's name, 30:4
typology.fulfillment: The full revelation of the Father and the Son, made known in Jesus Christ, John 1:18.

typology.type: The prayer for daily bread, not poverty or riches, 30:8
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' own teaching to pray, 'give us this day our daily bread,' Matthew 6:11.

study.christ: Agur's rhetorical, deliberately unanswerable question, 'what is His name, and what is the name of His Son, surely you know,' verse 4, finds its true and only adequate answer in Jesus Christ, the Son who has made the Father known, John 1:18, so that what remained a mystery even to a wise man's honest confession of ignorance is finally revealed in the incarnation. And Agur's simple prayer, neither poverty nor riches but the bread that is his portion, verse 8, anticipates the Lord's own teaching to His disciples to ask their Father for exactly this daily provision, Matthew 6:11, showing how deeply consistent the Bible's vision of contented, trusting dependence on God really is.
study.god: God is the Holy One whose full knowledge exceeds any human wisdom, verse 3, yet every word He speaks is utterly flawless and a shield to all who take refuge in Him, verse 5. He is also the God who hears the simple, honest prayer for daily bread, wanting to spare His people both the arrogance riches can breed and the desperation poverty can provoke, verses 8 to 9.

crossReferences.note: The Son has made the Father known, the fullest answer to Agur's question in Proverbs 30:4.
Give us this day our daily bread, echoing Agur's prayer in Proverbs 30:8.
God's rhetorical questions to Job parallel Agur's questions about who has measured creation, Proverbs 30:4.
The word of the LORD is flawless, a shield, echoing Proverbs 30:5.

study.oneStory: Agur confesses the limits of human wisdom before the incomprehensible God, trusts completely in God's flawless word, and prays simply for daily bread rather than poverty or riches. His haunting, unanswered question about the name of God's Son finds its full and glorious answer centuries later in Jesus Christ, through whom the Father is finally and fully made known.

quiz.q: What does Agur confess about himself in verses 2 to 3?

options.text: That he is the most ignorant of men and has no knowledge of the Holy One.
options.note: Agur begins his oracle with humble self-assessment rather than a claim to superior wisdom.

options.text: That he possesses more wisdom than any other man.
options.note: This is the opposite of his actual confession.

options.text: That he understands the natural world perfectly.
options.note: His confession is about spiritual and theological ignorance, not merely natural science.

options.text: That he is moderately wise but still has room to grow.
options.note: His language is much stronger, calling himself the most ignorant, not merely moderately wise.

quiz.q: What does verse 5 say about every word of God?

options.text: It is flawless, and He is a shield to those who take refuge in Him.
options.note: Confidence in God's word grounds Agur's confessed personal ignorance.

options.text: It requires human correction and refinement.
options.note: This is the opposite of calling God's word flawless.

options.text: It applies only to ancient Israel, not to all people.
options.note: The verse makes a universal claim about God's word, not a limited one.

options.text: It is mostly reliable but occasionally uncertain.
options.note: The verse's claim is absolute, 'flawless,' not partial reliability.

quiz.q: What two things does Agur ask for in verses 7 to 9?

options.text: That falsehood be kept far from him, and that he be given neither poverty nor riches, but his daily bread.
options.note: Agur prays for honesty and contented, sufficient provision.

options.text: Great wealth and a long life.
options.note: This is essentially the opposite of what Agur actually requests.

options.text: Forgiveness of sins and eternal life.
options.note: While these are legitimate biblical requests elsewhere, they are not what this specific prayer asks for.

options.text: Poverty alone, to prove his humility.
options.note: Agur specifically asks to avoid both poverty and riches, not to embrace poverty deliberately.

quiz.q: According to verses 24-28, what quality do the ant, rock badger, locust, and lizard share despite being small or weak?

options.text: They are exceedingly wise.
options.note: Wisdom in these small creatures is shown through fittingness to their calling, not strength or size.

options.text: They are the strongest creatures on earth.
options.note: The text specifically calls them creatures of little strength or power.

options.text: They are dangerous predators.
options.note: This is not the point being made about these creatures.

options.text: They are wise only because of their size advantage.
options.note: The passage's point is the opposite, that their wisdom is impressive precisely despite their small size or weakness.

quiz.q: What does verse 32 instruct if you have foolishly exalted yourself or plotted evil?

options.text: Put your hand over your mouth.
options.note: Self-restraint and silence are urged as the appropriate response to recognized folly or evil scheming.

options.text: Announce your plans publicly to gain support.
options.note: This is the opposite of the restraint the verse calls for.

options.text: Seek immediate revenge on your rivals.
options.note: The verse's counsel is self-restraint, not aggression toward others.

options.text: Wait a few days before taking further action.
options.note: The verse's specific instruction is silence, putting a hand over the mouth, not merely a delay before continuing.

questions: Agur begins by confessing his own ignorance before God, verses 2-3. Where do you need to admit the limits of your own understanding rather than pretending to have it all figured out?
Agur prays for neither poverty nor riches, only his daily bread, verse 8. Where is your own desire for either comfort or wealth putting your dependence on God at risk?
Agur's question about the name of God's Son, verse 4, is answered centuries later in Jesus. How does knowing the Son's name change the way you read Agur's honest confession of ignorance?
The small creatures of verses 24-28 are called wise despite being weak or small. Where might you be underestimating your own capacity to be faithful simply because you feel small or insignificant?

Verse 32 counsels silence, a hand over the mouth, after foolish self-exaltation or plotting. Is there a recent moment of pride or scheming in your own heart that calls for this kind of humble silence before God?

translationNotes.note: 'Agur son of Jakeh' and the recipients 'Ithiel' and 'Ucal' are obscure; some ancient versions and modern scholars read the Hebrew consonants differently, yielding 'I am weary, O God, I am weary and worn out,' as reflected in this BSB rendering, rather than treating Ithiel and Ucal as proper names throughout.

The rhetorical question 'What is His name, and what is the name of His Son' uses the Hebrew word ben, son, without further explanation in its immediate context, leaving its fullest meaning for later revelation to unfold.
'The leech has two daughters: Give and Give' is a proverbial image for insatiable demand, introducing the numerical list of things that are never satisfied that follows in verse 16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 30. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
